unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6: Natural Wonders and Conservation! In this unit, you will explore the extraordinary landscapes of East Africa and beyond, discovering how mountains, valleys, rivers, and wildlife shape the world around us. You will read an original adventure story called "The Citadel of Wind and Stone," set in the dramatic highlands of the Horn of Africa, where a group of young people must work together, make difficult decisions, and learn the meaning of true leadership. Through this story, you will encounter powerful vocabulary, practise important grammar structures, and develop your speaking and writing skills.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners exploring natural wonders and conservation decisions

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very cruel or terrible act
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
practiceSentences: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
The word atrocity belongs to our vocabulary group 1 vocabulary.
I can explain atrocity in my own words.
I can connect atrocity to this unit's texts.
I can use atrocity in a clear Grade 8 sentence.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'atrocity', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Natural Wonders and Conservation.

Form and use comparative adverb structures to compare actions (for example, "She completed the task more quickly than he did").

Construct sentences using the future passive voice (for example, "The report will be completed by Friday").
Convert direct speech into reported speech and report requests accurately.
Recognise and use zero conditionals (facts and general truths) and first conditionals (real future possibilities).

readings.title: Natural Wonders and Conservation: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 6: Natural Wonders and Conservation! In this unit, you will explore the extraordinary landscapes of East Africa and beyond, discovering how mountains, valleys, rivers, and wildlife shape the world around us. You will read an original adventure story called "The Citadel of Wind and Stone," set in the dramatic highlands of the Horn of Africa, where a group of young people must work together, make difficult decisions, and learn the meaning of true leadership. Through this story, you will encounter powerful vocabulary, practise important grammar structures, and develop your speaking and writing skills.
This unit is designed for self-paced learning. You will work through each section at your own speed, practising reading, listening, speaking, and writing every day. Your AI Tutor is available around the clock to help you with anything you find challenging, and you will also have two teacher sessions per week to discuss your progress, ask questions, and get personalised feedback.
The theme of this unit is natural wonders and conservation. As you work through the lessons, you will think about why it matters to protect the environment, how human actions affect natural landscapes, and what role leadership and teamwork play in solving problems. You will learn to express comparisons clearly, talk about possibilities and consequences, report what others have said, and describe future events using the passive voice. By the end of this unit, you will be a more confident, expressive, and thoughtful communicator in English.
Throughout this unit, you will build on the grammar and vocabulary skills you developed in earlier units. The grammar focus is on comparatives and superlatives, but you will also work with comparative adverbs, pronouns and quantifiers, zero and first conditionals, reported speech and requests, the future passive voice, and phrasal and prepositional verbs. Each of these grammar areas will help you communicate with greater precision and variety.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use comparative and superlative forms of adjectives correctly, including irregular forms like better, worse, best, and worst.
Form and use comparative adverb structures to compare actions (for example, "She completed the task more quickly than he did").
Understand and use pronouns and quantifiers such as anyone, nothing, few, and all in your writing and speech.
Construct sentences using the future passive voice (for example, "The report will be completed by Friday").
Convert direct speech into reported speech and report requests accurately.
Recognise and use zero conditionals (facts and general truths) and first conditionals (real future possibilities).
Identify and use common phrasal and prepositional verbs such as get through, back down from, stand up to, and give in to.
Read and comprehend the story "The Citadel of Wind and Stone" and discuss its themes of leadership, teamwork, and conservation.
Use all 31 vocabulary words from this unit accurately in your own sentences, conversations, and written work.
Write structured paragraphs and short essays that incorporate comparatives, superlatives, conditionals, and reported speech.
Speak confidently about natural wonders, conservation, and personal experiences using the grammar and vocabulary from this unit.
Reflect on your own learning and set goals for continued improvement.
Before you begin reading the story, it is important to familiarise yourself with the key vocabulary for this unit. These words appear throughout the story and the grammar exercises. Knowing them in advance will make your reading smoother and your understanding deeper.
Below is a preview of some of the most important words. You will find the complete vocabulary list with definitions, parts of speech, and example sentences in the separate Vocabulary document. For now, look through these words and think about whether you have encountered any of them before.
Group A — Words About People and Behaviour
Group B — Words About Actions and Conflict
Group C — Words About Places and Nature
There are 31 vocabulary words in total for this unit. Make sure you study all of them using the Vocabulary document. Try to learn five or six words each day, and practise using them in sentences.

readings.title: Natural Wonders and Conservation: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In this unit, you will study seven grammar topics. The primary focus is on comparatives and superlatives, which allow you to compare people, places, things, and actions. You will also study several supporting grammar structures that will help you express yourself with greater range and accuracy. Here is a brief introduction to each topic. Full explanations, examples, and exercises are in the Grammar document.
Comparatives help you compare two things. You form them by adding -er to short adjectives (tall becomes taller) or by using more before longer adjectives (beautiful becomes more beautiful). Superlatives help you compare three or more things to show the highest degree. You form them by adding -est (tallest) or using most (most beautiful). For example: "The Simien highlands are higher than the coastal plains" and "Mount Everest is the tallest mountain in the world."
Some adjectives do not follow the regular pattern. The most important irregular forms are good / better / best and bad / worse / worst. You must memorise these because they do not take -er or -est. For example: "Her plan was better than his" and "That was the worst storm this year."
Adverbs describe how an action is done. To compare actions, you use more or less with adverbs of two or more syllables. For one-syllable adverbs, add -er. For example: "She climbed more carefully than he did" and "He runs faster than his brother." Irregular adverbs like well become better: "She speaks English better than before."
Pronouns replace nouns to avoid repetition. Quantifiers tell you how much or how many. Key words include anyone, nothing, few, a few, all, and some. Understanding the difference between few (negative, meaning not many) and a few (positive, meaning some) is especially important. For example: "Few students attempted the climb" versus "A few students reached the summit."
The future passive voice is used to describe actions that will be done to something or someone. The structure is: Subject + will + be + past participle. For example: "The trail will be cleared by the rangers" and "New trees will be planted along the river." This is useful when you want to focus on the action rather than who performs it.
Reported speech allows you to tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. You change the tense and pronouns. For example, direct speech: "I am going to the citadel." Reported speech: "She said she was going to the citadel." For requests, you use asked or told plus to and the base verb: "He asked them to stop."
Zero conditionals express facts and general truths: If + present simple, present simple. For example: "If you heat water to 100 degrees, it boils." First conditionals express real future possibilities: If + present simple, will + base verb. For example: "If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the hike." These are essential for talking about consequences and possibilities.
Phrasal verbs combine a verb with a preposition or particle to create a new meaning. The four key phrasal verbs in this unit are: get through (to complete or survive something), back down from (to withdraw from a position), stand up to (to resist or confront), and give in to (to surrender or yield). Learning these will help you understand natural, everyday English.
The centrepiece of this unit is the story "The Citadel of Wind and Stone." This is an original adventure story set in the highlands of the Horn of Africa, where a group of young students take part in a challenging leadership programme. The story explores themes of leadership, teamwork, resilience, conservation, and ethical decision-making.
Before you open the Story document, take a few minutes to think about the following questions. You can write brief answers in your notebook or discuss them with your AI Tutor:
Now open the Story document and read "The Citadel of Wind and Stone" carefully. As you read, pay attention to the following:

readings.title: The Citadel of Wind and Stone, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Theme: Conflict, decision-making, and resilience under pressure
Setting: The Simien Highlands, northern Ethiopia — Horn of Africa
Genre: Adventure drama with psychological and ethical tension
The mountain trail twisted like a coiled rope through the Simien Highlands, dust rising in swirls around the students' boots. Their destination lay far above — a stone fortress locals called "the Citadel of Wind and Stone." It had once protected an ancient kingdom, and now it served as the final challenge in a national youth leadership programme.
Samira adjusted the straps on her pack and glanced behind. The trail team was still together, though some were slowing. The air thinned as they climbed, and the path grew rougher. To reach the citadel was no small task — it was grueling, designed to test not just physical endurance, but judgment.
"We need a rest soon," said Isaac, panting. "The sun is hitting harder now."
"If we stop too often," Samira replied, "we will not make it by nightfall."
Musa, who had taken the lead early, scoffed. "Some of you are too soft. I could do this in half the time."
His tone was always brusque, bordering on arrogant. He had already clashed with others several times, but he was undeniably proficient — fast, clever, and athletic. Yet Samira watched him with caution. His actions were often too bold, too fast, too audacious.
Still, the group moved on, following a gnarled ridge where the wind began to howl. Eddies of dust and air spun off the edges, making the cliffs feel alive.
They reached a small respite beneath a rock outcrop by midday. The group huddled in the shade, eating dried dates and roasted chickpeas.
"This whole hike feels like a punishment," muttered Nadia, one of the younger students. "What if someone gets hurt?"
"If they do," Musa said, "they should not have come. No one forced them."
Samira frowned. "We are a team. If one fails, we all fail. You were told that."
Musa stood abruptly. "Then you had better keep up."
As he strode ahead, others murmured.
"He will get us into trouble," Ishmael said. "The guides warned us. If we split, we are responsible for what happens."
"If he antagonizes a guide," another added, "they might confiscate our radios."
"We cannot afford that," Samira said. "Let us catch up — but carefully."
An hour later, they reached a sharp bend where the path narrowed along a steep drop. Musa stood at the edge, arms crossed.
"What are you doing?" Samira called.
"There is a shortcut through that ravine. Less distance."
"That was not on the map," she warned.
"It will get us there faster."
He stepped forward. "I am going."
"If you do," she said firmly, "you will break the rules. That is a transgression we agreed to avoid."
Musa hesitated. But pride surged in his chest. "I will meet you there."
Before anyone could stop him, he leapt down the slope and disappeared into the ravine's shadow.
Nadia quavered, her voice shaking. "What if he falls?"
"If he does," Samira said, her voice steady, "we go after him. But not now. He made a choice."
They took the long route — slower but safer.
By late afternoon, the citadel came into view. It stood high atop a ridge, stone walls wrapped around jagged rock like a crown. The final climb was steep and exposed. Wind pushed against them, deterring even the strongest from rushing.
The path forked. One route went around a sheer cliff — longer, but protected. The other crossed a narrow bridge of rock — fast but perilous.
"The short one looks... outlandish," Ishmael said.
"Musa would take it," someone muttered.
Samira did not respond. She studied the terrain, noting conspicuous signs of erosion near the narrow bridge. Stones were loose. A crack split the path near the end.
"We take the long path," she decided.

readings.title: The Citadel of Wind and Stone, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
They moved cautiously. Wind tore at them, but the trail held. They reached the citadel gates as the sun dipped behind the mountains. An instructor greeted them.
"You made it — intact," he said. "Any sign of Musa?"
The instructor's face grew dark. "He was warned not to break formation."
"He took a shortcut through the ravine. He said he would meet us here."
The instructor radioed the outposts. No one had seen him. Concern flickered.
Night fell. The stone walls echoed with silence. Samira could not sleep. Her mind spun with questions.
Just before dawn, a call came through: "Found him. Minor injury. He is being escorted back."
By sunrise, Musa limped into the courtyard, clothes torn and covered in dust.
"I tripped near a crevice," he admitted. "It took hours to climb out."
"You are lucky," the instructor said. "A few more steps, and the path would have crumbled."
"I was trying to show I could lead," Musa murmured. "But I failed."
"You were reckless," Samira said, not unkindly. "Leadership is not proving you are the fastest. It is knowing when to wait."
He looked at her, shame in his eyes.
That afternoon, the group was debriefed. Each student had to present a reflection.
Nadia stood first. "I learned that fear can be wise. That sometimes the most crucial choice is to wait."
Isaac followed. "I thought I was strong until I realised strength comes from others."
Samira stepped forward. "I have always been cautious. But I learned to trust instinct and facts. I did not want to command — I wanted us all to arrive."
"I was wrong. I mistook daring for wisdom. I thought rules were limits. But now I see — they are tools."
Applause filled the citadel's stone walls.
As they descended the mountain the next day, wind trailing behind them, the group stayed close. No shortcuts. No prideful sprints. They stopped to help each other. They moved like a single thought.
At the base, a village child offered them water. Samira handed over her bottle in return. The child smiled.
"Not tired," Samira answered. "Just finished something important."
If Musa had reached the citadel first, he might have claimed victory — but learned nothing.
If Samira had given in to pride, the group might have fractured.
But they did not. And that made all the difference.
atrocity, destination, desist, deter, authority, anarchy, furtive, antagonize, kindle, audacious, quaver, deplore, transgression, astute, gnarled, resent, outlandish, culminate, respite, conspicuous, claim, proficient, exploit, brusque, confiscate, eddy, citadel, headway, crucial, grueling, ardent
Comparatives and superlatives (faster, the strongest, more cautious)
Irregular comparative forms (better, worse, best, worst)
Comparative adverbs (more carefully, more quickly)
Pronouns and quantifiers (some, anyone, few)
Future passive voice (He is being escorted back)
Reported speech (He said he would meet us here)
Zero and first conditionals (If we stop too often, we will not make it; If one fails, we all fail)
Phrasal and prepositional verbs (give in to, stand up to, back down from, get through)

grammar.title: How to Form Comparatives
grammar.explanation:
The way you form a comparative depends on the length of the adjective:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -er to the end. Example: tall becomes taller, fast becomes faster, old becomes older.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The way you form a comparative depends on the length of the adjective:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -er to the end. Example: tall becomes taller, fast becomes faster, old becomes older.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: Change the -y to -i and add -er. Example: happy becomes happier, easy becomes easier, busy becomes busier.
Adjectives with two or more syllables: Place more before the adjective. Example: beautiful becomes more beautiful, difficult becomes more difficult, interesting becomes more interesting.
After the comparative, use than to introduce the second item in the comparison. For example: "The mountain trail is steeper than the coastal path."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form comparatives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form Superlatives
grammar.explanation:
Superlatives follow a similar pattern:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -est to the end. Example: tall becomes the tallest, fast becomes the fastest.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Superlatives follow a similar pattern:
One-syllable adjectives: Add -est to the end. Example: tall becomes the tallest, fast becomes the fastest.
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: Change the -y to -i and add -est. Example: happy becomes the happiest, easy becomes the easiest.
Adjectives with two or more syllables: Place most before the adjective. Example: beautiful becomes the most beautiful, difficult becomes the most difficult.
Always use the before a superlative. For example: "Mount Kilimanjaro is the tallest mountain in Africa."
Spelling Rules
If a one-syllable adjective ends in a single vowel followed by a single consonant, double the consonant before adding -er or -est. Example: big becomes bigger / biggest, hot becomes hotter / hottest.
If an adjective ends in -e, just add -r or -st. Example: large becomes larger / largest.
If an adjective ends in -y, change the -y to -i before adding -er or -est.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form superlatives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes
grammar.explanation:
Double marking: Do not use "more" and "-er" together. Say "taller," not "more taller." Say "most beautiful," not "most beautifulest."
Using comparatives for three or more items: Use superlatives, not comparatives, when comparing three or more things. Say "She is the tallest of the three," not "She is the taller of the three."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Double marking: Do not use "more" and "-er" together. Say "taller," not "more taller." Say "most beautiful," not "most beautifulest."
Using comparatives for three or more items: Use superlatives, not comparatives, when comparing three or more things. Say "She is the tallest of the three," not "She is the taller of the three."
Forgetting 'the' before superlatives: Always include "the" before a superlative. Say "the best," not just "best."
Examples from the Story
"His actions were often too bold, too fast, too audacious." (Here, comparisons are implied through repetition.)
"The sun is hitting harder now." (Comparative adverb)
"The most crucial choice is to wait." (Superlative)
Exercise 1A — Fill in the Blanks
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
The Simien Mountains are __________ (high) than the hills near the coast.
This is the __________ (beautiful) sunset I have ever seen.
Samira is __________ (careful) than Musa when making decisions.
The highland air is __________ (thin) than the air at sea level.
Of all the routes, the long path was the __________ (safe).
Today's weather is __________ (hot) than yesterday's.
The citadel is the __________ (impressive) building in the region.
Walking is __________ (slow) than running, but it is also __________ (steady).
Answer Key — Exercise 1A
higher
most beautiful
more careful
thinner
safest
hotter
most impressive
slower / steadier
Topic 2: Irregular Comparative Forms
What This Rule Means
Some adjectives do not follow the regular rules for forming comparatives and superlatives. These are called irregular forms, and you must memorise them. The most common irregular forms are:
Examples
"Samira's plan was better than Musa's shortcut idea."
"The weather today is worse than it was yesterday."
"This is the best solution for the whole team."
"That was the worst storm the highlands had seen in years."
"The second campsite is farther from the citadel than the first."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes
grammar.explanation:
Do not say "gooder" or "goodest" — always use better and best.
Do not say "badder" or "baddest" — always use worse and worst.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Do not say "gooder" or "goodest" — always use better and best.
Do not say "badder" or "baddest" — always use worse and worst.
Do not say "more good" or "most good" — these forms are incorrect.
Exercise 2A — Fill in the Blanks
My grandmother's cooking is __________ (good) than the school canteen's.
This maths problem is __________ (bad) than the last one.
Of all the students, Samira is the __________ (good) at leading the team.
That test was the __________ (bad) I have ever taken.
The village is __________ (far) from the city than I thought.
He ate __________ (little) food today than yesterday.
Answer Key — Exercise 2A
better
worse
best
worst
farther / further
less
Topic 3: Comparative Adverb Structures
What This Rule Means
Comparative adverbs help you compare how actions are performed. Just as you compare adjectives (bigger, more beautiful), you can compare adverbs (faster, more carefully). This allows you to describe differences in how people do things.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Form Comparative Adverbs
grammar.explanation:
One-syllable adverbs: Add -er. Example: fast becomes faster, hard becomes harder.
Adverbs of two or more syllables (usually ending in -ly): Use more or less before the adverb. Example: quickly becomes more quickly, carefully becomes more carefully, quietly becomes less quietly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One-syllable adverbs: Add -er. Example: fast becomes faster, hard becomes harder.
Adverbs of two or more syllables (usually ending in -ly): Use more or less before the adverb. Example: quickly becomes more quickly, carefully becomes more carefully, quietly becomes less quietly.
Irregular adverbs: Well becomes better, badly becomes worse, far becomes farther or further.
Examples
"Samira climbed more carefully than Musa."
"He completed the trail faster than anyone expected."
"She spoke less audibly when she realised others were listening."
"Isaac works harder than most of the other students."
"Samira speaks English better than she did last year."
Exercise 3A — Complete the Sentences
She can run __________ (quick) than her friend.
He speaks __________ (quiet) when he is thinking.
They completed the project __________ (efficient) than expected.
Musa moved __________ (reckless) than the rest of the group.
The team worked __________ (hard) on the second day than on the first.
Answer Key — Exercise 3A
more quickly
more quietly
more efficiently
more recklessly
harder
Topic 4: Pronouns and Quantifiers
What This Rule Means
Pronouns are words that replace nouns to avoid repetition (he, she, it, they, anyone, nothing). Quantifiers tell you how much or how many of something there is (few, a few, all, some, every, none). Using these correctly makes your language clearer and more natural.
Key Pronouns and Quantifiers
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to form comparative adverbs in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes
grammar.explanation:
Confusing few and a few: "Few" has a negative sense (not many), while "a few" is more positive (some, enough).
Confusing anyone and everyone: "Anyone" means any single person (it does not matter which), while "everyone" means all people without exception.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Confusing few and a few: "Few" has a negative sense (not many), while "a few" is more positive (some, enough).
Confusing anyone and everyone: "Anyone" means any single person (it does not matter which), while "everyone" means all people without exception.
Using nothing when you mean anything: In negative sentences, use "anything." Say "I do not have anything," not "I do not have nothing" (which is a double negative).
Exercise 4A — Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct word: anyone, nothing, few, a few, all, everyone.
__________ can answer this question if they think carefully.
I have __________ to do today — I finished everything yesterday.
__________ of the players scored a goal — only two out of fifteen.
__________ the cookies are gone! Someone ate the last one.
__________ enjoyed the trip — not a single person complained.
__________ students volunteered to stay late and help clean up.
Answer Key — Exercise 4A
Anyone
nothing
Few
All
Everyone
A few
Topic 5: Future Passive Voice
What This Rule Means
The future passive voice is used when you want to focus on the action being done rather than the person doing it. It describes something that will happen to the subject in the future.
Structure
Subject + will + be + past participle (+ by + agent)
The "by + agent" part is optional. You include it if you want to say who will perform the action. You leave it out if the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious.
Examples
"The trail will be cleared by the rangers."
"New trees will be planted along the river."
"The results will be announced tomorrow."
"The homework will be completed by the students."
"A presentation will be given by a local expert."
When to Use the Future Passive Voice
When the action is more important than who does it: "The bridge will be repaired next month."
When the doer is unknown: "The decision will be made soon."
In formal writing, such as reports and announcements: "Prizes will be awarded at the ceremony."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Comparing Natural Wonders
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose two natural wonders that you know about (for example, the Simien Mountains and the Great Rift Valley, or the Nile River and the Indian Ocean). Prepare a short speech of one to two minutes in which you compare them using comparatives and superlatives. Try to include at least five comparison sentences. Here are some ideas to get you started:
"The Simien Mountains are higher than the hills near my town."
"The Nile is the longest river in Africa."
"The Indian Ocean is more vast than any lake I have ever seen."
"The weather on the highlands is colder than on the coast."
"Of all the natural wonders I have learned about, the Great Rift Valley is the most fascinating."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise comparing natural wonders and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Role Play: The Leadership Debate
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine you are one of the characters in the story. With a partner, family member, or your AI Tutor, act out a debate about whether Musa's decision to take the shortcut was right or wrong. One person argues in favour of taking risks, and the other argues in favour of caution and teamwork. Use conditionals in your arguments:
"If Musa had waited, he would not have been injured."
"If we always play it safe, we will never discover anything new."
"If you split from the group, you put everyone at risk."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise role play: the leadership debate and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Reporting What Others Said
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise reported speech by telling a partner or your AI Tutor what the characters in the story said. For example:
Musa said that he could do the hike in half the time.
Samira told the group that they were a team.
The instructor said that Musa had been warned not to break formation.
Nadia said that she had learned that fear can be wise.
After practising with story examples, try reporting real things that people around you have said today. For example: "My mother told me that dinner would be ready at seven."
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise reporting what others said and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Conservation Discussion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Prepare a two-minute talk on one of the following topics. Use vocabulary from this unit and try to include at least two conditionals and two comparatives:
Why is it important to protect natural environments like mountains and forests?
What are the biggest threats to wildlife in East Africa?
What can young people do to help with conservation efforts?
Describe a natural place you have visited or would like to visit. What makes it special?
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you be my debate partner? I want to argue that caution is better than risk-taking.
Or: Can you listen to my two-minute talk about conservation and give me feedback?
Or: Can you help me practise reported speech by having a conversation I can then report?
Writing
Strong writing requires planning, drafting, and revising. In this section, you will practise several types of writing, all connected to the themes and grammar of Unit 6. Remember to write in complete sentences, use paragraphs, and include the grammar structures you have learned.
Writing Task 1 — Comparative Paragraph
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise conservation discussion and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
Develop this into a sustained five- to six-minute response with synthesized evidence, source evaluation, a counterargument and answers to follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary, evidence, reasoning and audience effect.

writing.title: Writing 1: Comparative Paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a paragraph of 80 to 120 words comparing two places you know. These could be two cities, two schools, two parks, or two natural features. Use at least five comparative or superlative forms. Here is an example opening:
"My grandmother's village is smaller than the city where I live, but it is also more peaceful. The air there is cleaner, and the nights are quieter. Of all the places I have visited, it is the most beautiful."
After writing your paragraph, underline every comparative and superlative form you used. Check that you have used at least five. Then ask your AI Tutor to review your paragraph and suggest improvements.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.title: Writing 2: Conditional Sentences
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write eight conditional sentences — four using the zero conditional and four using the first conditional. Your sentences should relate to the themes of nature, conservation, or leadership. For example:
Zero: "If you cut down too many trees, the soil erodes."
First: "If we plant more trees this year, the forest will grow back."
After writing your sentences, label each one as "Zero" or "First." Check that you have used the correct verb tenses in each clause.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.title: Writing 3: Reported Speech Transformation
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Below are five direct speech sentences. Rewrite each one in reported speech. Pay careful attention to tense changes and pronoun changes.
Musa said: "I am the fastest climber in the group."
Samira said: "We need to stay together."
The instructor said: "You must not leave the trail."
Nadia asked: "What will happen if someone gets lost?"
Isaac said: "I have never seen mountains this high before."
Write your answers in your notebook, then check them against the answer key in the Grammar document or ask your AI Tutor to verify them.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.title: Writing 4: Short Essay: Conservation Matters
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short essay of 150 to 200 words on the following topic:
"Why is it important to conserve natural environments, and what can young people do to help?"
Your essay should have three paragraphs:
Introduction: State your main argument. Why does conservation matter?
Body: Give at least two specific reasons or examples. Use comparatives, conditionals, and vocabulary from this unit.
Conclusion: Summarise your argument and suggest one action young people can take.
Use at least five vocabulary words from this unit in your essay. After writing, read your essay aloud to check that it flows well. Then share it with your AI Tutor for feedback.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.title: Writing 5: Future Passive Voice Paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine that your school is planning an environmental conservation day. Write a paragraph of 60 to 80 words describing what will happen, using the future passive voice for at least four sentences. For example:
"Trees will be planted in the school garden. Rubbish will be collected from the nearby river. A presentation about endangered animals will be given by a local expert. Posters will be displayed in every classroom."
After writing, underline every future passive construction and make sure each one follows the pattern: subject + will + be + past participle.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you check my comparative paragraph and tell me if I used at least five comparatives correctly?
Or: Can you help me rewrite direct speech as reported speech? I want to practise.
Or: Can you give me feedback on my conservation essay and suggest ways to improve it?
Activities and Exercises
This section contains a range of exercises to help you consolidate what you have learned. Work through them carefully, checking your answers as you go. If you get stuck, revisit the Grammar and Vocabulary documents, or ask your AI Tutor for help.
Exercise 1 — Comparative and Superlative Forms
Complete each sentence with the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
The highland trail is __________ (difficult) than the coastal path.

writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy

writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

activities.title: Activities 1: Vocabulary Matching
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word on the left with its correct definition on the right. Write the letter of the definition next to the word number.
atrocity        a) very important or necessary for something to happen
deter            b) a very cruel or terrible act
crucial          c) to make someone decide not to do something
anarchy          d) showing strong feelings or enthusiasm
ardent           e) a situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion
Answers: 1-b, 2-c, 3-a, 4-e, 5-d
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Word Families
activities.instructionsAndItems: Many vocabulary words belong to word families. Knowing related forms helps you use them in different contexts. Complete the table below:
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Vocabulary Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write one original sentence for each of the following words. Your sentences should show that you understand the meaning of the word. Try to connect your sentences to the themes of nature, conservation, or leadership.
destination
audacious
conspicuous
grueling
confiscate
kindle
deplore
culminate
headway
citadel
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Vocabulary in Paragraphs
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write a short paragraph (50 to 80 words) using at least six vocabulary words from this unit. Your paragraph should be about one of these topics:
A challenging outdoor adventure
A leader you admire and why
Why it is important to protect natural environments
After writing, underline the vocabulary words you used and check that each one is used correctly. Share your paragraph with your AI Tutor for feedback.
🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor
Try asking: Can you test me on the definitions of ten vocabulary words from Unit 6?
Or: Can you give me a paragraph with blanks where I need to fill in the correct vocabulary word?
Or: Can you help me use the word 'culminate' in three different sentences?
Successful language learning depends on consistent daily practice. Here are some strategies to help you get the most out of this unit and continue building your English skills between teacher sessions.
Tip 1 — Use a Vocabulary Notebook
Keep a dedicated notebook for new vocabulary. For each word, write the word, its part of speech, the definition, and an example sentence that you create yourself. Review your notebook every evening before bed. Research shows that reviewing words just before sleep helps your brain store them in long-term memory.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt

activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

quizzes.question: What does 'atrocity' mean?
quizzes.options: A very cruel or terrible act | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To make someone decide not to do something | To stop doing something
quizzes.correctAnswer: A very cruel or terrible act
quizzes.explanation: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.

quizzes.question: What does 'destination' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where someone is going or something is being sent | A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To make someone decide not to do something
quizzes.correctAnswer: A place where someone is going or something is being sent
quizzes.explanation: Our destination for the field trip is the national park.

quizzes.question: What does 'desist' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To stop doing something
quizzes.explanation: The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.

quizzes.question: What does 'deter' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words | To make someone decide not to do something
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make someone decide not to do something
quizzes.explanation: The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.

quizzes.question: What does 'authority' mean?
quizzes.options: Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words | To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
quizzes.correctAnswer: The power or right to give orders and make decisions
quizzes.explanation: The park ranger has the authority to close the trail if conditions are unsafe.

quizzes.question: What does 'anarchy' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | Bold and daring; willing to take risks | To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words | To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
quizzes.correctAnswer: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion
quizzes.explanation: After the leader resigned, the organisation fell into anarchy.

quizzes.question: What does 'furtive' mean?
quizzes.options: Bold and daring; willing to take risks | Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | To shake or tremble while speaking, often from nervousness | To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
quizzes.correctAnswer: Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed
quizzes.explanation: He gave a furtive glance at the map before anyone noticed.

quizzes.question: What does 'antagonize' mean?
quizzes.options: Bold and daring; willing to take risks | To feel or express strong disapproval of something | To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words | To shake or tremble while speaking, often from nervousness
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words
quizzes.explanation: If you keep interrupting, you will only antagonize the speaker.

quizzes.question: What does 'kindle' mean?
quizzes.options: A wrong action that breaks a rule or law | To feel or express strong disapproval of something | To shake or tremble while speaking, often from nervousness | To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
quizzes.correctAnswer: To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin
quizzes.explanation: Reading about the Simien Mountains kindled her interest in conservation.

quizzes.question: What does 'audacious' mean?
quizzes.options: A wrong action that breaks a rule or law | Able to understand things quickly and make good decisions | Bold and daring; willing to take risks | To feel or express strong disapproval of something
quizzes.correctAnswer: Bold and daring; willing to take risks
quizzes.explanation: The audacious explorer crossed the ravine without any safety equipment.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A very cruel or terrible act The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.
A place where someone is going or something is being sent Our destination for the field trip is the national park.
To stop doing something The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.
To make someone decide not to do something The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.
The power or right to give orders and make decisions The park ranger has the authority to close the trail if conditions are unsafe.
A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion After the leader resigned, the organisation fell into anarchy.
Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed He gave a furtive glance at the map before anyone noticed.
To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words If you keep interrupting, you will only antagonize the speaker.
To start a fire, or to make a feeling or interest begin Reading about the Simien Mountains kindled her interest in conservation.
Bold and daring; willing to take risks The audacious explorer crossed the ravine without any safety equipment.
